coredata.dc:title: Real-time monitoring of NIR-triggered drug release from phase-changeable nanodroplets by photoacoustic/ultrasound imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier GmbH.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier GmbH.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yang
Dumani, Diego S.
Hallam, Kristina A.
Emelianov, Stanislav Y.
Ran, Haitao
coredata.dc:description: Optical-responsive nanodroplets have recently been studied as a new mode of remotely controlled drug delivery. As a class of new emerging smart drug carriers, NIR-absorber-loaded perfluorocarbon nanodroplets can be converted into gas bubbles through laser stimulation, called optical droplet vaporization (ODV), which provides a potential strategy to deliver therapeutic agents to solid tumors on demand. However, there is a lack of suitable technologies to monitor these drug-loaded nanodroplet behaviors in vivo, and control the site and amount of drug released. In this study, ultrasound and photoacoustic imaging technology were applied to directly monitor optical-responsive, drug-loaded nanodroplets within the tissue. We explored the effects of laser energy, repetition rate, and number of pulses on the release profiles of the delivered drug as well as ultrasound and photoacoustic imaging signal-intensity curves. The conducted studies demonstrated that this noninvasive technology helped determine the optimum time point for laser activation on accumulated drug-loaded nanodroplets within tissues, allowing for the potential to effectively treat pathologies while minimizing drug-related toxicities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoacoustic/ultrasound imaging
Perfluorocarbon nanodroplets
Optical droplet vaporization
Controlled drug release

In vitro drug release and cell viability from nanodroplet vaporization
Ex vivo measurements of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization and drug release
In vivo examination of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
Preparation and characterization of NIR responsive nanodroplets
US/PA signals of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
In vitro drug release in NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
Ex vivo measurements of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization and drug release
In vivo monitoring of NIR-triggered droplet vaporization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Size distribution of nanodroplets. Inside: a) nanodroplets without curcumin (b) Curcumin-loaded nanodroplets.
In vitro PA/US imaging of nanodroplets after dilution at 1/1000 at 1064 nm in the tube phantom: (A) PA imaging, (B) Average PA intensity, (C) US imaging, (D) Average US intensity.
Relative released drug proportion from nanodroplets.
Relative viability of cells incubated with drugs released from nanodroplets for 24 h.
A, US/PA imaging of ex vivo spleen. B, US intensity of ex vivo spleen. C, PA intensity of ex vivo spleen.
A, Ex vivo spleen images using non-linear contrast mode US imaging. B, Average intensity of non-linear contrast mode US images.

A,US/PA imaging of the spleen in vivo, with each US/PA imaging session lasting 60 s. B-D, Average photoacoustic signal of the spleen in vivo for each imaging session (B, 10 mJ/cm2. C, 15 mJ/cm2. D, 20 mJ/cm2).

head.ce:title: Real-time monitoring of NIR-triggered drug release from phase-changeable nanodroplets by photoacoustic/ultrasound imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optical-responsive nanodroplets have recently been studied as a new mode of remotely controlled drug delivery. As a class of new emerging smart drug carriers, NIR-absorber-loaded perfluorocarbon nanodroplets can be converted into gas bubbles through laser stimulation, called optical droplet vaporization (ODV), which provides a potential strategy to deliver therapeutic agents to solid tumors on demand. However, there is a lack of suitable technologies to monitor these drug-loaded nanodroplet behaviors in vivo, and control the site and amount of drug released. In this study, ultrasound and photoacoustic imaging technology were applied to directly monitor optical-responsive, drug-loaded nanodroplets within the tissue. We explored the effects of laser energy, repetition rate, and number of pulses on the release profiles of the delivered drug as well as ultrasound and photoacoustic imaging signal-intensity curves. The conducted studies demonstrated that this noninvasive technology helped determine the optimum time point for laser activation on accumulated drug-loaded nanodroplets within tissues, allowing for the potential to effectively treat pathologies while minimizing drug-related toxicities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Noninvasive photoacoustic/ultrasound imaging technology used for real-time monitoring NIR-triggered drug release from phase-changeable nanodroplets attributed to optical droplet vaporization behavior.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tumor-targeted chemotherapy is a promising strategy to overcome the problems encountered with current cancer treatments [1–4]. However, the efficacy of chemotherapy is hindered by dose-limiting toxicity, which is associated with nonspecific biodistribution of the anticancer agents, leading to a low therapeutic index and unwanted side effects [2–4]. Therefore, novel nanomedicines have been developed to selectively respond to tumor-specific conditions and perform on-demand drug release, providing a means to increase the concentration of drugs in the tumor. In particular, novel nanomedicines include stimuli-responsive drug carriers that can release their payload locally in tumor tissue under the action of endogenous stimuli, such as pH, enzymes, or redox response; or exogenous stimuli, such as heat, light, or ultrasound (US) [5]. Moreover, local application of external stimuli requires precise information about the tumor location, which can be obtained via imaging prior to and during treatment. Imaging can also be useful to monitor the biodistribution of the nanocarrier, as well as drug release kinetics. Real time imaging can thus be used to control the timing of the external stimulus application for optimal results [6,7].

Laser or light is a promising external triggering mechanism for the nanocarriers, because it allows a more precise control of the time and location of the drug release event, with minimal side effects to the surrounding tissue, compared with other methods such as ultrasound or heating [8–10]. Despite its advantages, UV–vis (400 −700 nm) light has a limited penetration depth and may cause damage to susceptible tissues in the skin or eyes. On the other hand, near-infrared (NIR) irradiation penetrates deeper in tissues, and it is preferred to minimize thermal damage to healthy tissues [8,11,12]. Thus, NIR laser-activated nanocarriers can be triggered more selectively and in deeper target tissues.

The application of light as an external stimulus allows the use of various types of NIR contrast agents during the nanocarrier synthesis. Recently, laser-activated phase-change droplets made with high boiling point perfluorocarbons (PFCs) and NIR-photoabsorbers, such as dyes or other nanoparticles, have been explored [13,14]. When exposed to an NIR laser beam, the absorbed light energy is converted into heat, inducing droplet phase transition from liquid nanodroplets to gas microbubbles: an event called optical droplet vaporization. Upon pulsed laser irradiation, the laser-activated perfluorocarbon nanodroplets (PFCnDs) can be rapidly vaporized, resulting in fast drug release. In addition, photoacoustic (PA) imaging can be used to detect the location of the PFCnDs and track the acoustic waves generated by the vaporization (i.e., drug release) events in real time [13–18]. Photoacoustic imaging is a noninvasive imaging modality that provides sufficient resolution, contrast, and depth range for the real time monitoring of PFCnDs [15–18].

In this manner, the use of light as an external stimulus can serve two purposes: to trigger the drug delivery events, and to be used as part of the photoacoustic imaging real time monitoring system. Although systemically-injected nanocarriers would reach both normal tissues and target sites, the localized light irradiation would provide spatially-controlled drug release. By distributing optically-responsive systems that are only activated in an on-demand manner at the target sites, the side effects of the loaded drugs may be mitigated [19].

Perfluorocarbon gas bubbles are clinically used as ultrasound contrast agents. Thus, the loaded nanodroplets can be tracked via photoacoustic imaging before and during the vaporization and drug release, and the laser-activated phase-transitioned nanodroplets can be visualized using ultrasound imaging.

In this study, we employed NIR-responsive phase-shift nanodroplets made with perfluoropentane as drug carriers (PFCnDs). Duo to the relatively low boiling point of perfluoropentane, the nanodroplets are liquid at body temperature prior to activation, and remain in gas form once they are vaporized. Curcumin, a lipophilic polyphenol extracted from the herb Curcuma longa L., was used as the model drug. Curcumin has been demonstrated to have several types of medical benefits, including anticancer, anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties [20–22]. The therapeutic action mechanisms of curcumin include the inhibition of lipid peroxidation, free radical-scavenging activity (such as scavenging of reactive oxygen species), down regulation of inducible nitric oxide synthase activity, suppression of NF-κB activation, stimulation of apoptosis via upregulation of p16 and p53, among others [23,24]. The curcumin-loaded PFCnDs were activated, and the delivery of drug was monitored. The amount of drug released from nanodroplets was approximately calculated by analyzing the signal intensity variations of the photoacoustic/ultrasound images before, during, and after laser irradiation. The resulting NIR-responsive drug-loaded nanodroplets have a potential as on-demand theranostic agents for image-guided controlled drug delivery.

Synthesis of drug-loaded PFCnDs was modified from our previously developed methods [25]. Briefly, 2.0 mg Epolight 3072 dye (absorption maximum at 1054 nm, Epolin) and 2.0 mg curcumin (Sigma) were added to a mixture of1200 μl PBS and 600 μl of 1% v/v Zonyl FSO fluorosurfactant (Sigma). The solution was vortexed for 15 s. Two hundred microliters of perfluoropentane (PFP, FluoroMed L.P.) were added and sonicated in an ice-cold ultrasonic water bath (VWR, 180 W) for 5 min. The solution was centrifuged at 100 rcf for 1 min to remove any excess dye. The supernatant was taken and further washed at 1000 rcf for 5 min. Drug-loaded nanodroplets were obtained. The optical absorption of drugs was measured on a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 220, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The hydrodynamic size of PFCnDs was characterized with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern).

The ultrasound and photoacoustic (US/PA) imaging system used is shown in Fig. 1. All imaging experiments were conducted using a 20-MHz ultrasound probe (MS-250,Visualsonics Inc.) and two laser fiber bundles coupled to a US/PA imaging system (Vevo 2100,Visualsonics Inc.) combined with an external pulsed laser. The pulsed Nd:YAG laser (5–7 ns, 20 Hz PRF, Tempest 30) was operated at a wavelength of 1064 nm. Each image is reconstructed from four consecutive laser pulses using the Vevo 2100 proprietary algorithm. Images were post processed and analyzed using MATLAB. Non-linear contrast mode US imaging was performed with the same equipment, by acquiring harmonic signals related to the presence of bubbles in tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: US/PA images of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro experiments were performed using a poly(acrylamide) tissue mimicking tube phantom with a pipette inclusion, constructed as previously described [17]. Briefly, 40% acrylamide solution was mixed with water, ammonium persulfate and tetramethylethylenediamine. The mixture was poured into a mold and allowed to solidify around a pipette fixed in place, creating an inclusion within the mold. PFCnDs were diluted 1:1000 in PBS. The diluted PFCnDs were drawn up into a transfer pipette and placed in the inclusion mold. US/PA imaging was performed with the previously described system at a frame rate of 5 frames per second.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release and cell viability from nanodroplet vaporization
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the influence of laser fluence on curcumin-loaded nanodroplets, the diluted nanodroplet solution was injected into the tube phantom and irradiated by the NIR laser. The collection of samples after laser irradiation was centrifuged at 1000 rcf for 5 min to measure the concentration of released curcumin in the supernatant solution. The released drug concentration was determined at 420 nm by UV–vis absorbance. The efficiency of drug release was calculated based on the percentage between the sample with and without laser exposure (control).

MDA-MB-231 human breast cancer cells were routinely cultured in high glucose DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C and 5%CO2. MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 2 × 104 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C, 5%CO2 for 24 h. To test the viability of PFCnDs under various laser irradiation conditions, the cells were exposed to fresh culture medium containing the PFCnDs. After 4 h of incubation, the cells were irradiated by the NIR laser at 1064 nm for 60 s and incubated for another 12 h. Cytotoxicity was measured using the MTT assay according to the manufacturer's suggested procedures. The viability percentage was expressed as absorbance in the presence of drug-loaded nanodroplets as a percentage of that of the vehicle control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex vivo measurements of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization and drug release
ce:section.ce:para: All experimental protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at Georgia Institute of Technology. Mice were anesthetized using a combination of 1.5–2.5% isoflurane and O2. For all in vivo studies, 100 μl of curcumin-loaded PFC nanodroplets were injected intravenously through the jugular vein. After PFCnDs were allowed to circulate for 20 min, the spleen was collected and placed on top of a polyacrylamide phantom base. US/PA images of the ex vivo spleen model were collected (Fig. 1). After laser exposure, 1 g of spleen tissue was weighed and normal saline was added to prepare the tissue for homogenization. The extracted drug concentration was determined by high-performance liquid chromatography.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo examination of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
ce:section.ce:para: As described above, mice were anesthetized using a combination of 1.5–2.5% isoflurane and O2. One hundred microliters of curcumin-loaded PFC nanodroplets were injected intravenously through the jugular vein. After PFCnDs were allowed to circulate for 20 min, US/PA images of the spleen were obtained. Vaporization behavior of PFCnDs over the span of a 5-minute imaging session was collected, and this imaging procedure was performed four more times, with a 10 min interval in between each imaging session.

All experiments were performed in triplicate, and the results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analyses of the experimental data from different groups were performed by applying one-way ANOVA. A value of p < 0.05 was considered significant, and p < 0.01 was considered highly significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of NIR responsive nanodroplets
ce:section.ce:para: PFC nanodroplets were prepared with Zonyl FSO fluorosurfactant (Sigma) shells and perfluoropentane (FluoroMed, L.P.) cores. Epolight 3072 dye (Epolin, Inc.) with an absorption maximum at 1054 nm was selected as the photoabsorber. The model drug curcumin has been used in traditional Chinese medicine and in the food industry. Curcumin has been demonstrated to have several medical benefits, such as anticancer, anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties [20]. As shown in Fig. 2, after loading curcumin, PFCnDs displayed a dark yellow color, which primarily resulted from the addition of curcumin. Droplets without curcumin loading were showed in white. The drug encapsulation efficiency and drug loading content were 85.36 ± 4.2% and 1.15 ± 0.04%, respectively, measured through curcumin absorbance at 420 nm. The size of the PFC nanodroplets was 343.4 nm ± 16.8 nm. Due to the tumor enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, the cutoff size of the gap junctions (380–780 nm) between vascular endothelial cells would allow these PFC carriers to passively extravasate into tumor tissue to improve intratumoral drug accumulation [26,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: US/PA signals of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
ce:section.ce:para: A tissue mimicking tube phantom with a pipette inclusion containing PFCnDs was used to investigate the phase transition of nanodroplets. Pulsed laser irradiation at 1064 nm with different laser fluences was used to vaporize PFCnDs for 60 s. The photoacoustic and ultrasound signals were both recorded throughout the vaporization. The average photoacoustic ( Fig. 3. B) and ultrasound (Fig. 3. D) signals inside the pipette were plotted as a function of laser irradiation time. Since PFP liquid with low boiling temperature could be easily vaporized, the photoacoustic signals showed a descending trend for all groups. The initial laser pulses generated high photoacoustic signals, and the subsequent pulses generated smaller signals. This behavior suggests that most of the nanodroplets were vaporized during the initial laser pulses, and the enhanced PA signal was due to the pressure waves from the vaporization events. Once the initial vaporization has occurred, a constant signal is still present due to the photoabsorber in the PFCnDs. When using low fluences such as 5 mJ/cm2 or 10 mJ/cm2, the initial vaporization peak is negligible suggesting that very little vaporization is occurring. This behavior can be beneficial to track the PFCnDs in vivo and confirm their location prior to vaporization.

As a result of PFCnD vaporization, enhanced ultrasound contrast could also be observed. The ultrasound contrast initially increased until it reached a stable value. When using higher laser fluences, the ultrasound signals increased rapidly and reached a higher steady state value, suggesting that laser irradiation played a predominant role in inducing NIR responsive nanodroplet vaporization. In this case, even the lowest fluence of 5 mJ/cm2 caused a slight increase in ultrasound contrast, showing that some vaporization occurred. The energy threshold at which the laser is predominantly used for imaging vs. image-guided vaporization must be determined on a case-by-case basis, considering the depth of the target lesion, the type of photoabsorber and perfluorocarbon used, the wavelength and the laser repetition frequency.

The enhanced ultrasound signals mainly came from bubbles generated during laser irradiation. Different laser fluences showed various liquid-phase transitions. The ultrasound signals were plotted as a function of different laser irradiation time points. The slope of the ultrasound curves is shown in Fig.S1. The curves decreased sharply and became flat at the time point marked by arrows in Fig.S2. For high laser fluence over 20 mJ/cm2, the first 30 s of laser exposure could trigger most droplets. A longer irradiation time was required for lower fluences to activate the same quantity of droplets. As a result, the trends in US/PA behavior show that drugs loaded within PFCnDs can be selectively released from PFCnDs during their vaporization, allowing for US/PA controlled drug release and monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release in NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization
ce:section.ce:para: PFCnD delivery of curcumin was examined in vitro to evaluate drug release efficacy from nanodroplets during laser activation ( Fig. 4). This demonstrated that the amount of released drugs during the initial laser irradiation increased with laser fluence. At 5 mJ/cm2, over 35% of curcumin was released in the initial 10 s of laser irradiation. The remaining curcumin released in the following 10 second intervals was similar in their relative released proportion. When it reached 25 mJ/cm2, the initial 10 s of laser irradiation triggered approximately 70% of the drugs to be released from the nanocarriers. In the following laser irradiation, however, it showed different drug release kinetics from that of the 5 mJ/cm2 groups. For PFCnDs, loaded drugs were released during the droplet-to-bubble transition under the action of the laser. The change in drug release with respect to laser fluence enables the ability to manipulate the manner in which drugs are locally released. For example, in a disease where rapid release of drugs is needed, higher fluences could be used. On the other hand, lower fluence values would be most useful for sustained release of a particular therapeutic. As a result, PFCnD delivery can be designed based on the application’s specific needs.

To assess the tumoricidal potential of curcumin-loaded PFCnDs, the cytotoxicity of the PFCnDs was evaluated using human breast cancer cell lines ( Fig. 5). When cocultured with nanocarriers, tumor cells were killed by curcumin released from NIR-responsive nanodroplets. The cell viability of the control groups without laser irradiation was over 80%, which is a clear indication of the very low cytotoxicity of the nanodroplets. In nanodroplets, the drug (curcumin) was localized in the shell formed by the stabilizing polymers. Although not exposed to a laser, cells could be killed by therapeutic drugs from the surface of the nanodroplets. The cell viability of the 25 mJ/cm2 groups was 20% lower than that of the control groups in the first 10 s of laser irradiation. Cell viability decreased with laser fluence as well as irradiation time. Based on Fig. 4, high laser fluencies, such as 25 mJ/cm2, could trigger over 70% of the drugs released from nanocarriers, resulting in low cell viability in this group. On the other hand, the irradiation time could affect cell viability as well. As shown in Fig. 5, as the laser exposure time increased, the cell viability decreased due to drug release during the phase transition of PFC nanodroplets. This result suggested that high laser energy could activate most nanodroplet vaporization, inducing the release of many drugs. It is preferred for some diseases that require rapid and fast drug release in chemotherapy. However, low laser fluency seems to have sustained drug release profiles. Therefore, an external laser stimulus could be used as a tool to obtain a predicting drug release kinetic in chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex vivo measurements of NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization and drug release
ce:section.ce:para: An ex vivo model was used to investigate NIR-triggered nanodroplet vaporization and drug release behaviors. After injecting curcumin-loaded PFCnDs intravenously, the spleen was collected, and photoacoustic and ultrasound imaging was performed ( Fig. 6, Fig.S3). The spleen was chosen as an ideal organ for imaging due to its role in the circulation and excretion of PFCnDs from the body. The spleen was irradiated with different laser fluences. The photoacoustic and ultrasound imaging were both enhanced, indicating vaporization of PFCnDs within the ex vivo organ. Furthermore, the initial laser pulses vaporized most of the nanodroplets in the spleen, resulting in the highest photoacoustic signals. Unlike the in vitro study, different laser irradiation times were required to achieve peak photoacoustic signals, with the peak PA signal being achieved more quickly at higher laser fluences. This is mainly because the laser energy was not high enough to trigger nanodroplet vaporization. The PA signal in tissue was reduced due to the absorption and scattering of light within the tissue, which was less significant in vitro and phantom studies. Heating was the main mechanism of photoacoustic effects. Due to the comprehensive nature of true tissue, the heating efficiency of the laser was reduced. Therefore, additional laser time is required to achieve the highest imaging signals. Additionally, the photoacoustic signal decreased with laser time and fluency (Fig. 6). On the contrary, the enhanced ultrasound imaging increased with laser time ( Fig. 7). After laser irradiation, all tissues displayed high echogenicity, indicating the vaporization of PFCnDs within the image plane. The contrast ultrasound signal increased significantly. The US signal after laser irradiation was increased approximately 4.5-fold after 20 mJ/cm2. The results confirmed that laser irradiation could induce PFCnD nanodroplet vaporization, generating photoacoustic and enhanced ultrasound imaging. Laser-activated PFCnDs provide localized enhanced contrast within the tissue, which can be used to monitor the release of therapeutics into the tissue.

Controlled release of curcumin was also studied in the ex vivo model (Fig.S4). The results showed a similar trend to the one seen in the in vitro study (Fig. 5). After 60 s of laser irradiation, less than 4 µg of curcumin was released from the PFCnDs at a laser fluence of 10 mJ/cm2. For laser fluences of 15 mJ/cm2 and 20 mJ/cm2, the curcumin released was approximately three to four times higher than the one using 10 mJ/cm2. Higher laser fluences showed the ability to vaporize more PFCnDs in the 60-second laser irradiation interval. The fluence dependence of PFCnDs shows that the release of drug in the tissue can be modulated by laser fluence and monitored via US/PA imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo monitoring of NIR-triggered droplet vaporization
ce:section.ce:para: For in vivo experiments, the spleen was selected as the target organ. Fig. 8 and Fig.S5 shows the US/PA imaging results after five imaging sessions. US signal enhancement from droplet vaporization increased as the number of imaging sessions increased. For 10 mJ/cm2, the US signal increased until the third imaging session, when droplet signals began to saturate in the tissue, resulting in a steady US signal, which meant no additional droplets were being vaporized on site. Unlike the ex vivo model, vaporized droplets during in vivo imaging have the capability to circulate and potentially leave the tissue. As a result, achieving stable US contrast within the spleen requires multiple laser irradiation or US/PA imaging sessions. In the case of 15 mJ/cm2 and 20 mJ/cm2, US signals continued to increase after 5 imaging sessions. In vivo PA signals showed a similar behavior to the ex vivo experiments. Specifically, an increase in PA signal was shown in the first imaging session followed by a continued decrease in signal over the other imaging sessions for all laser fluences. The peak PA signal occurred more quickly at higher fluences. As mentioned before, the optimal laser fluences to either image (track) or vaporize (image-guided drug release) the PFCnDs must be determined on a patient-specific basis, considering tissue characteristics such as depth, optical properties, and the contrast agent characteristics such as boiling point and optical absorption spectrum. Overall, the developed NIR-responsive nanodroplets could be an excellent, triggered drug release vehicle. Additionally, this study suggested that US/PA imaging could be used to monitor droplet vaporization and predict drug release behaviors upon laser irradiation.

In this study, optically responsive perfluoropentane nanodroplets (PFCnDs) were used as a remotely controlled drug delivery system. By using an NIR laser stimulus, drug-loading carriers were activated within target sites. The release of drug payloads within the droplets could be spatially controlled due to the optically triggered vaporization of the droplets. This controlled release has the potential to reduce unwanted side effects caused by systemic drug delivery. Additionally, using optical droplet vaporization, the phase transition of PFCnDs can be monitored by ultrasound/photoacoustic imaging. Laser-activated PFCnDs can provide real-time imaging signals, allowing for monitoring prior, during, and after drug release from PFCnDs that is controlled by laser fluence and irradiation time. The fluence and time thresholds for a particular rate of drug release will vary depending on the laser repetition frequency, tissue characteristics, and contrast agent characteristics. Thus, fluence and irradiation time must be tuned on a patient-specific basis. Overall, this research demonstrated that NIR-activated PFCnDs are effective drug delivery carriers with potential use in image-guided, laser-mediated drug delivery.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0120), Senior Medical Talents Program of Chongqing for Young and Middle-aged, and the Kuanren Talents Program of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared that no competing interest exists.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.pacs.2023.100474.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyprodrug amphiphiles: hierarchical assemblies for shape-regulated cellular internalization, trafficking, and drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Hu, J. Hu, J. Tian, et al. Polyprodrug amphiphiles: hierarchical assemblies for shape-regulated cellular internalization, trafficking, and drug delivery, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 135 (2013) 17617–17629.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cell-penetrating hyperbranched polyprodrug amphiphiles for synergistic reductive milieu-triggered drug release and enhanced magnetic resonance signals

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tumor-targeting and pH-sensitive lipoprotein-mimic nanocarrier for targeted intracellular delivery of paclitaxel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.Y. Santiesteban, K.A. Hallam, S.K. Yarmoska, et al. Color-coded perfluorocarbon nanodroplets for multiplexed ultrasound and photoacoustic imaging, Nano Research 12 (2019) 741–747.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Hobbs, W.L.Monsky, F. Yuan, et al. Regulation of transport pathways in tumor vessels: Role of tumor type and microenvironment. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA. 95 (1998) 4607–4612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect in tumor vasculature: the key role of tumor-selective macromolecular drug targeting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Maeda. The enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect in tumor vasculature: the key role of tumor-selective macromolecular drug targeting. Adv Enzyme Regul 41 (2001) 189–207.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para:
Image 1
Yang Cao is the associate professor of institute of ultrasound imaging, second affiliated hospital, Chongqing Medical University, China. His research work is mainly about developing application of focused ultrasound and photoacoustic imaging-guided therapy, new types of nanoparticles for tumor diagnostic and therapy, novel nanostructures for photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, sonodynamic therapy, creating new approaches for delivering multiply therapeutic drugs, and imaging contrast agents in controlled manners, activated by physical stimuli, such as ultrasound, light or magnetic fields.

Image 2
Diego S. Dumani received his PhD in biomedical engineering from Georgia Institute of Technology and Emory University School of Medicine in 2018. Currently, he is an associate professor at the University of Costa Rica, where he conducts research in the areas of medical imaging, instrumentation, contrast agents, and laser–tissue interaction.

Image 3
Kristina A. Hallam received a B.S.E. degree in biomedical engineering from Duke University, Durham, NC, USA, in 2014. She received her Ph.D. degree in biomedical engineering from Georgia Institute of Technology and Emory University School of Medicine, Atlanta, GA, USA in 2020. Her doctoral work explored the development and implementation of ultrasound and light responsive nanoagents for neurological applications.

Image 4
Stanislav Y. Emelianov is a Joseph M. Pettit Endowed Chair, Georgia Research Alliance Eminent Scholar, and professor of electrical and computer engineering and biomedical engineering at the Georgia Institute of Technology and Emory University School of Medicine. Furthermore, Dr. Emelianov is the Director of the Ultrasound Imaging and Therapeutics Research Laboratory. Projects in Dr. Emelianov’s laboratory are focused on the discovery, development, and clinical translation of diagnostic imaging and therapeutic instrumentation, augmented with theranostic nanoagents.

Image 5
Haitao Ran is the Director of Ultrasound Department of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Director of the Institute of Ultrasound Imaging, Chongqing Medical University, and Director of Chongqing Key Laboratory of Ultrasound Molecular Imaging. Dr. Ran’s laboratory are focused on the integration of ultrasound molecular imaging and diagnosis and treatment.
